title: Sorn
summaryShort: The world's first three-Michelin-starred Thai restaurant, an obsessive Southern Thai tasting menu by Chef Ice Supaksorn.
summaryLong:
Sorn is the most important Thai restaurant on the planet right now, and the first ever to hold three Michelin stars. Chef Supaksorn 'Ice' Jongsiri opened it as a love letter to the heat-and-funk traditions of Southern Thailand, and the restaurant has spent every year since refining that vision rather than diluting it.

The long tasting menu is built almost entirely from heritage ingredients: aged budu fish sauce, hand-pounded curry pastes, indigenous chillies, line-caught local crab, dry-aged Sri Trang chicken. Dishes look almost rustic but are calibrated to the gram, and the kitchen sources directly from southern fishing villages and farms.

Diners come for what is genuinely a once-in-a-trip experience: a small dining room, a single seating, and a meal that feels less like fine dining theatre and more like a kitchen showing you exactly how good Thai food can be when no shortcut is taken.
whySpecial: No other Thai restaurant has ever held three Michelin stars. Sorn proved that uncompromising regional Thai cooking, served on its own terms, belongs at the very top of global fine dining.
priceNote: Tasting menu approx THB 8,500++ per person
tags: michelin, three-star, fine-dining, thai, southern-thai, tasting-menu, sukhumvit, ekkamai

title: Sühring
summaryShort: Twin chefs Thomas and Mathias Sühring reinterpret their German family recipes inside a beautiful 1970s Bangkok villa.
summaryLong:
Sühring is the rare fine-dining room that feels like a home, because it actually was one. Identical twin chefs Thomas and Mathias Sühring took over a leafy mid-century Bangkok villa and turned it into a personal museum of modern German cooking, drawing on their grandmother's recipe books and their training under Juan Amador and at Mezzaluna.

The long tasting menu walks through Schwarzwald, Bavaria, the Baltic and Berlin: smoked eel from the Spree, sourdough fermented in-house, käsespätzle redone with brown butter and aged comté, sauerbraten reduced into glassy layers, jet-black sour-cherry desserts. The pairings dig deep into German Riesling and Spätburgunder, a wine list almost no one else in Asia is building.

It is one of Bangkok's most consistently booked dining rooms, beloved equally by visiting Europeans missing home and by Thai diners who know this is the most considered take on the cuisine in the region.
whySpecial: Two-Michelin-star modern German cuisine served inside a private villa: an utterly unique format, with one of Asia's deepest German wine cellars to match.
tags: michelin, two-star, fine-dining, german, european, tasting-menu, sathorn, yannawa

title: Le Du
summaryShort: Chef Ton's seasonal modern Thai tasting menu, a multi-time Asia's 50 Best winner that put progressive Thai cooking on the global map.
summaryLong:
Le Du, hidden down a Silom soi, is where Chef Thitid 'Ton' Tassanakajohn made his name. A New York-trained, sommelier-certified Thai chef, he opened Le Du in 2013 with a then-radical idea: contemporary fine dining built from Thai farm produce, with French technique sitting underneath rather than on top.

The seasonal four- and six-course menus rotate hard. Expect river prawn with khao kluk kapi reimagined as a single luminous bite, sticky rice with foie gras, betel leaf cured fish, slow-cooked Thai beef with jaew. The wine pairing is one of the smartest in Bangkok, leaning into natural and grower bottles that hold up to chilli and fermentation.

Le Du has been Asia's 50 Best No.1 and is a staple of every serious Bangkok itinerary. Despite the accolades it remains a small, focused, surprisingly relaxed room.
whySpecial: The restaurant that proved modern Thai could win Asia's 50 Best No.1, run by the most influential Thai chef of his generation.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, modern-thai, tasting-menu, silom, asia-50-best

title: Nusara
summaryShort: Chef Ton's love letter to his grandmother Nusara, served in a shophouse with sweeping views of Wat Pho.
summaryLong:
Sister restaurant to Le Du, Nusara is the more personal project of Chef Thitid 'Ton' Tassanakajohn. Set in a restored shophouse in the old town with the spires of Wat Pho framed in the windows, it is dedicated to his late grandmother, whose recipes form the spine of the menu.

The twelve-course tasting reads like a Bangkok-Thai family album: crab fried rice elevated with house-cured roe, beef massaman braised for days, deep-South curries thickened with fresh coconut cream, classic Thai desserts plated like jewellery. Where Le Du is technical, Nusara is emotional.

It has been a fixture in Asia's 50 Best top ten and is one of the hardest reservations in the city, partly because the room only seats around forty and partly because the view at sunset is genuinely one of the best in Bangkok.
whySpecial: A Thai grandmother's recipe book, rebuilt at fine-dining precision, served above the rooftops of the old town with Wat Pho in view.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, modern-thai, heritage, phra-nakhon, old-town, view

title: Potong
summaryShort: Chef Pam Pichaya's progressive Thai-Chinese tasting menu inside her family's 120-year-old apothecary in Chinatown.
summaryLong:
Potong occupies the five-story shophouse where Chef Pichaya 'Pam' Soontornyanakij's family ran a Chinese herbal pharmacy for more than a century. She kept the patina, restored the staircases, and turned the building into one of Bangkok's most atmospheric fine-dining destinations.

The twenty-course menu is built around the philosophy of five flavours, five textures, five techniques: dim sum reborn as caviar-topped tartlets, aged duck breast glazed with Chinese plum, slow-fermented tofu, traditional Teochew desserts re-engineered. Every course is a quiet conversation with her family's heritage.

Pam was named Michelin Thailand's first-ever female chef to win Opening of the Year alongside her star, and Potong is now firmly on Asia's 50 Best, drawing diners deep into Chinatown for what may be Bangkok's most narrative-driven meal.
whySpecial: Five generations of Chinese-Thai apothecary history, served as a 20-course tasting in the building where it all happened.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, thai-chinese, progressive, yaowarat, chinatown, asia-50-best

title: Chef's Table
summaryShort: Two-star French haute cuisine on the 61st floor of lebua, with skyline views and a famously serious wine programme.
summaryLong:
Chef's Table sits high in the State Tower's lebua complex, the same building that became famous for the Sky Bar in The Hangover Part II. Where Sirocco draws the crowds, Chef's Table is the quiet, jacket-required room above it, holding two Michelin stars year after year.

The kitchen, led by Chef Vincent Thierry, executes classical French haute cuisine with a luxe Asian inflection: caviar pavé, blue lobster, Wagyu, glassy reductions, immaculate pastry. The wine cellar is one of the deepest in Thailand, with verticals of grand cru Burgundy and Bordeaux that few rooms in Asia can match.

It is the destination Bangkok choice for an old-school fine-dining French evening with a view that doesn't quit.
whySpecial: Two-Michelin-star classical French cooking, 220 metres above the Chao Phraya, with a wine cellar most Paris three-stars would envy.
tags: michelin, two-star, fine-dining, french, sathorn, lebua, view, wine

title: Mezzaluna
summaryShort: Lebua's flagship two-star European tasting room, perched 65 floors above the river under a domed glass ceiling.
summaryLong:
Mezzaluna is the second of lebua's two-star kitchens and arguably the more theatrical. The dome-ceilinged dining room sits 65 floors up, with the river bending below and a long open kitchen at the centre.

Chef Ryuki Kawasaki, a Tokyo-born veteran of Joël Robuchon and Pierre Gagnaire, runs an eight-course modern European tasting that leans Japanese in precision and seasonality: Hokkaido uni with caviar, Bresse pigeon, foie gras XO, line-caught Mediterranean fish flown in twice a week. The pairings draw from one of Asia's largest cellars.

It is the rare Bangkok room where the food, the room, the view and the service are all operating at the same elevated level.
whySpecial: Two Michelin stars, 65 floors up, under a glass dome with a Tokyo-trained French-trained chef cooking some of the most precise food in the city.
tags: michelin, two-star, fine-dining, european, japanese-french, sathorn, lebua, view

summaryShort: A locavore Modern Thai room in Charoenkrung, built on 80% Thai ingredients and 20% playful global influence.
summaryLong:
80/20 is named after its founding rule: at least 80% of every dish must come from Thailand. Chef Saki Hoshino and Chef Napol 'Joe' Jantraget run it out of a converted shophouse in Charoenkrung, in the cluster of art galleries and indie bars that has become Bangkok's coolest food district.

The menu is small, fermentation-heavy and constantly changing: koji-aged proteins, hand-pressed Thai oils, foraged herbs, house-made vinegars, Thai charcuterie. There's a strong Japanese sensibility behind the Thai ingredients, courtesy of Chef Saki's pastry training.

It is one of the most distinctive Modern Thai kitchens in the country, and probably the most exciting tasting menu in Charoenkrung.
whySpecial: A radical 80% Thai sourcing rule, a fermentation lab in the kitchen, and one of Bangkok's most quietly inventive tasting menus.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, modern-thai, locavore, fermentation, charoenkrung

title: Nahm
summaryShort: The legendary traditional-Thai restaurant founded by David Thompson, still anchoring the COMO Metropolitan in Sathorn.
summaryLong:
Nahm is one of the most historically important Thai restaurants of the modern era. Founded by Australian chef David Thompson, it was the first Thai restaurant in the world to earn a Michelin star (in its London incarnation in 2002), and the Bangkok branch has held a star nearly every year since opening.

The menu pulls from old palace and regional manuscripts: relishes with elaborate garnishes, bone-in southern curries, ceremonial rice, rare Thai desserts. Nothing is dumbed down for international palates and the chilli register can be punishing in the best way.

Under Chef Pim Techamuanvivit (and now her successors) Nahm has become a beloved institution, set inside the COMO Metropolitan with a small pool-side terrace.
whySpecial: The first Thai restaurant in the world to win a Michelin star, still serving rigorous old-palace recipes most younger chefs have never even heard of.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, thai, traditional-thai, sathorn, como, hotel-restaurant

title: Wana Yook
summaryShort: A Modern Thai counter restaurant on Charoenkrung built around contemporary takes on regional Thai dishes.
summaryLong:
Wana Yook is one of the newer entries into Bangkok's Michelin Thai canon, set inside the heritage Central: The Original Store building on Charoenkrung. The room is intimate, with a counter looking onto the kitchen and a small dining room behind.

The tasting menu plays in Modern Thai territory: hand-cut Thai charcuterie, smoked river fish with herb pastes, slow-braised beef in muslim-style massaman, intricate Thai sweets to close. Plating is contemporary but every flavour is rooted firmly in Thai tradition.

It is the kind of place that earns a Michelin star quickly because the technique, sourcing and storytelling are all already in lockstep.
whySpecial: A young Modern Thai counter restaurant in a heritage Charoenkrung building, with an intimate chef-driven format.
tags: michelin, one-star, fine-dining, modern-thai, charoenkrung, counter, chef-driven

title: Côte by Mauro Colagreco
summaryShort: Argentine-Italian three-Michelin-star chef Mauro Colagreco's Riviera-inspired tasting menu, set on the Chao Phraya inside Capella Bangkok. Promoted to two MICHELIN stars in 2025 and retained for 2026.
summaryLong:
Côte transports the produce-led, garden-driven philosophy that earned Mauro Colagreco three stars at Mirazur (Menton) to a riverside dining room at Capella Bangkok. The menu reads like a love letter to the French and Italian Rivieras, with citrus, olive oil, fresh herbs and Andaman seafood replacing some of the Mediterranean catch.

Meals unfold through a Sun, Sea and Soil structure: vegetable-forward openers from Capella's farm partners, raw fish and crustaceans pulled from the Gulf and the south of France, and slow-cooked proteins finished with Riviera sauces. Service is relaxed-luxurious, in the Capella house style, with a long wine list pouring biodynamic French and Italian estates.

The room itself is one of the most atmospheric in Bangkok: floor-to-ceiling river windows, a lazily turning ceiling fan, and a quietly excellent open kitchen. Côte was promoted to two MICHELIN stars in the 2025 guide and held both stars for 2026.
whySpecial: The only Bangkok restaurant from a chef whose home base sits at the very top of the global fine-dining ladder, in arguably the city's most beautiful riverside dining room.
priceNote: Tasting menus from ~THB 6,500++ at lunch, ~THB 9,500++ at dinner
tags: michelin, 2-star, french, riviera, mauro colagreco, capella, riverside, fine dining

title: R-Haan
summaryShort: Two-Michelin-star Thai fine dining built around regional 'wisdom of Thai cookery,' served on hand-crafted royal-style serveware in a teakwood villa on Thong Lo.
summaryLong:
R-Haan is one of the most ambitious modern Thai restaurants in Bangkok, founded in 2018 around a simple thesis: that Thai cuisine deserves the same reverence as a Kyoto kaiseki or a Parisian tasting menu. Chef Chumpol Jangprai builds menus that travel north, north-east, central and south, holding to original recipes and ingredients prescribed by the regional canon.

The set-menu structure mirrors a traditional Thai meal: rice as the centerpiece, with curries, salads, soups and stir-fries arrayed around it. Each course is plated on commissioned brass and ceramic ware that nods to royal court dining. The wine and tea pairings have become quietly excellent over the past few seasons.

The restaurant occupies a teakwood villa just off Sukhumvit 53, with a private dining room for up to 20. Two MICHELIN stars retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: One of the very few Thai restaurants serving an uncompromised regional menu at this level of polish, with no fusion concessions to a Western palate.
tags: michelin, 2-star, thai, royal thai, regional, fine dining, thong lo

title: Gaa
summaryShort: Chef Garima Arora's two-Michelin-star progressive Indian tasting menu, blending her heritage with seasonal Thai produce in a teakwood Thai house on Sukhumvit 53.
summaryLong:
Gaa earned its second MICHELIN star in 2024, making Garima Arora the first Indian female chef to hold two stars. After moving from Soi Langsuan to a beautifully restored Thai wooden house off Sukhumvit 53, Gaa kept its philosophy intact: progressive Indian cooking that uses Thailand as a pantry and India as a memory.

The tasting menu is built around dishes you can almost recognise (a paratha, a kebab, an achaar) but executed with techniques and pairings that reframe the original. Garima trained at Noma and Verrat, and the ferment-and-fire DNA of those kitchens is unmistakable. Wine pairings lean toward small-grower European and Indian labels.

A 14-seat counter and a small dining room set the pace; the upstairs terrace doubles as a bar for after-meal chai and amari. Two MICHELIN stars retained in 2026.
whySpecial: The most internationally significant Indian restaurant in South-East Asia, run by one of the most awarded female chefs working today.
priceNote: Gaa Experience tasting menu from ~THB 5,200++ per guest
tags: michelin, 2-star, progressive indian, garima arora, fine dining, sukhumvit

title: Baan Tepa
summaryShort: Chef Chudaree 'Tam' Debhakam's two-Michelin-star modern Thai tasting menu, served inside her family's three-generation Bangkok home with a working culinary garden.
summaryLong:
Baan Tepa is built around a simple, hard-to-execute idea: cook a high-end seven-course tasting menu using only Thai ingredients, much of it grown or fermented on site. The setting is the Debhakam family home, a 1980s house that Chef Tam has converted into a kitchen, garden and dining space called Tepa.

The menu rotates with the harvest. A signature dish might be a hand-rolled khanom jeen with seasonal river fish, or a charcoal-grilled vegetable course pulled from beds you can see from your seat. Tam trained at Quintonil and runs one of the most sustainability-serious kitchens in the city: composting, no-waste fermentation, and zero air-freighted protein.

Only forty seats per night, three seatings, no walk-ins. Two MICHELIN stars retained in 2026.
whySpecial: A two-star tasting menu inside a family home, with a working garden you walk through on your way to the table — the most personal fine-dining room in Bangkok.
priceNote: Full Experience 7-course dinner ~THB 7,298 (service and tax included)
tags: michelin, 2-star, modern thai, sustainability, garden, female chef, fine dining

title: Anne-Sophie Pic at Le Normandie
summaryShort: Le Normandie at Mandarin Oriental Bangkok — Thailand's first French fine-dining restaurant — relaunched in 2025 under Anne-Sophie Pic, France's most-Michelin-starred living female chef.
summaryLong:
Le Normandie has been the benchmark for French fine dining in Bangkok since 1958. In late 2025 the Mandarin Oriental rebooted it as a partnership with Anne-Sophie Pic, the only French woman ever to hold three MICHELIN stars at her flagship in Valence and the most-starred female chef in the world.

The Pic style — long-cooked stocks, layered aromatics, vegetable-led plating — is brought to Bangkok by an executive team trained in her Valence kitchen. Tasting menus rotate with French and Thai seasons; signature Pic dishes such as the Berlingot pasta and Champagne-poached fish appear alongside new compositions written for the Bangkok pantry.

The room overlooks a sweeping bend of the Chao Phraya from the Mandarin Oriental's main wing, with the famous river-side dress code (jacket compulsory at dinner) intact. Two MICHELIN stars in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: Bangkok's oldest fine-dining French room, now run by the most decorated female chef in modern French gastronomy.
priceNote: Tasting menus from ~THB 6,800++ at lunch, ~THB 12,500++ at dinner
tags: michelin, 2-star, french, anne-sophie pic, mandarin oriental, riverside, fine dining

title: Aksorn
summaryShort: David Thompson's love letter to vintage Thai cookbooks, served in a 1950s former bookshop on Charoenkrung. One MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
After leaving Nahm, David Thompson — the Australian chef who arguably did more than anyone to preserve disappearing Thai recipes — opened Aksorn on the fifth floor of Central: The Original Store, a 1950 building that began life as Thailand's first imported-book retailer. The name means 'letters' or 'characters,' a nod to the building's literary past and to the cookbooks Thompson has collected for thirty years.

The prix-fixe rotates every few months around a different vintage Thai cookbook. You might be served a 1940s palace recipe one season, a regional north-east village dish the next. Plates are designed for the table, not the camera: punchy, unapologetic Thai flavour, served family style.

A rooftop bar handles aperitifs and digestifs with Thai-spirit-led cocktails. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The most intellectually serious Thai restaurant in Bangkok — every menu is a research project rooted in a single antique cookbook.
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, david thompson, heritage, charoenkrung, fine dining

title: AVANT
summaryShort: Singaporean chef Haikal Johari's intimate 10-seat counter on the 30th floor of Kimpton Maa-Lai, with one MICHELIN star and skyline views.
summaryLong:
AVANT marks Haikal Johari's return to Bangkok after almost a decade in Singapore, where he led several headline kitchens. The room is a discreet 10-seat low counter facing the open kitchen; behind it, the city skyline and Lumpini Park frame every course.

Haikal cooks contemporary French with confident Asian moves — Japanese stocks, Singaporean-Hokkien sambals, the occasional Thai herb — woven through classical technique. The set menu is short by design (six to eight courses) so each plate gets the attention it deserves, and the small room means the kitchen can adjust on the fly.

The wine list leans toward Burgundy, the Loire and small Australian producers, with sake and Japanese whisky for the Asian dishes. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: A 10-seat counter run by an internationally awarded chef — one of the most personal fine-dining rooms in the city.
tags: michelin, 1-star, french, asian-french, haikal johari, kimpton, counter dining

title: Coda
summaryShort: Chef Tap Supasit Kokpol's contemporary Thai tasting menu in a calm two-storey dining room at Sindhorn Village. Awarded its first MICHELIN star in 2025 and retained for 2026.
summaryLong:
Coda opened to a quietly euphoric reception from Bangkok's local food press, then collected its first MICHELIN star within a single guide cycle. Chef Tap reinterprets familiar Thai flavours through a minimalist, modern lens: dishes are precise, visually understated, and intensely seasoned in the Thai way without being heavy.

The space is two floors of a renovated villa inside Sindhorn Village. The downstairs lounge handles cocktails and aperitifs; upstairs is the tasting-menu room, capped at a small enough capacity that every course can be timed to the table. The MICHELIN inspectors specifically called out the 'expert seasoning, delicate textures, and confident Thai identity.'

Wine and sake pairings are unusually thoughtful for a restaurant this young. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The most exciting first-star kitchen of the 2025 cycle: minimalist plating and confident Thai flavour from a chef everyone in the city is now watching.
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, contemporary thai, sindhorn, fine dining, new

title: Gaggan
summaryShort: Gaggan Anand's reborn 14-seat counter — five-act, twenty-five-course theatre with shifting lights and a pulsing soundtrack. One MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Gaggan Anand opened his original Bangkok restaurant in 2010 and turned it into the most awarded Indian fine-dining establishment in Asia before closing it in 2019. The current Gaggan, around the corner from the original, is a 14-seat L-shaped counter where up to twenty-five inventive courses unfold across five theatrical acts.

Lights shift, the soundtrack pulses, and dishes arrive as edible jokes, provocations and homages — a signature 'Lick It Up' plate, fermentation experiments, and increasingly serious technique under the showmanship. The format is closer to a concert set list than a meal: a tightly rehearsed performance with the chef working the room.

At 14 seats Gaggan fills months in advance; pricing is at the very top of the Bangkok market and reservations are usually paid up front. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The most theatrical fine-dining experience in South-East Asia, run by the chef who put modern Indian cooking on the global map.
priceNote: ~THB 12,000++ for the menu; pairings extra; full prepayment usually required
tags: michelin, 1-star, progressive indian, gaggan anand, theatrical, counter dining, asoke

title: Raan Jay Fai
summaryShort: Auntie Jay Fai personally still cooks every dish at her ski-goggled wok station — a one-MICHELIN-star street stall world-famous for its crab omelette.
summaryLong:
Supinya 'Jay Fai' Junsuta has been cooking at the same Maha Chai Road shopfront for decades. Bangkok-locals queue for her wok-fried seafood; the tourist queue forms for her now-iconic crab omelette, a hand-shaped pillow of egg fully stuffed with sweet blue-crab meat. She wears ski goggles to protect her eyes from wok heat, and at 70-plus still personally cooks every dish.

The MICHELIN star she received in 2018 made Jay Fai the first street-food vendor in the world to hold one. She kept the star through every guide cycle since, including 2026. Bookings are notoriously hard, and the original walk-in queues stretch around the block.

The Chope reservation page below is the only official online booking channel — limited slots release at intervals. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The most famous street-food cook in the world, still working her own wok, in the same shopfront that earned her the star.
priceNote: Crab omelette ~THB 1,000-1,500; full meal ~THB 2,000-3,000 per person
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, street food, jay fai, old town, iconic

title: IGNIV Bangkok
summaryShort: Andreas Caminada's first IGNIV outside Switzerland — a fine-dining sharing concept at the St. Regis Bangkok, holding one MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
IGNIV (Romansh for 'nest') was conceived by Andreas Caminada, who runs a three-MICHELIN-star kitchen at Schloss Schauenstein in the Swiss mountains. Bangkok was the first international IGNIV when it opened on the ground floor of the St. Regis in 2020, and head chef Arne Riehn now runs the kitchen.

The concept inverts conventional fine dining. Instead of plated individual courses, guests work through a series of shared dishes — small bites, mains, sides, sweets — designed for the table. The technique is full Swiss-French haute cuisine; the format is closer to a long Sunday lunch with friends. Wine pairings draw on the IGNIV cellar in Bad Ragaz.

The room itself is one of the warmer corners of the St. Regis, with a quietly stylish dining counter facing the kitchen. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: Caminada-trained Swiss-French haute cuisine reformatted as a shared-table experience — elegant precision served like a feast.
priceNote: Sharing menus from ~THB 3,800++ at lunch, ~THB 5,800++ at dinner
tags: michelin, 1-star, european, swiss, caminada, st regis, sharing concept

title: Mia
summaryShort: Pongcharn 'Top' Russell and Michelle Goh's Asian-European tasting-menu restaurant in a two-storey townhouse — one MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Mia is run by chef-owners Pongcharn 'Top' Russell and Michelle Goh, a couple whose CVs include long stretches in Copenhagen, Singapore and London. The restaurant occupies a two-storey townhouse: a chic bar downstairs, three distinct dining rooms upstairs (one wallpapered in colourful William Morris prints, one tropical, one dark and intimate).

The seasonal 'Taste of Mia' menu shows off the duo's signature: complex, well-balanced flavours that work modern European technique through an Asian sensibility, with vegan and vegetarian options always offered. Pastry and bread are made in-house; the wine list leans natural and biodynamic with a strong English-language sommelier story.

One MICHELIN star, retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: One of the few one-star kitchens in Bangkok run by a chef couple who own the restaurant outright — every detail (rooms, plating, music, list) feels personal.
priceNote: Taste of Mia tasting menu from ~THB 3,800++
tags: michelin, 1-star, modern european, asian-european, chef couple, sukhumvit

title: Maison Dunand
summaryShort: Chef Arnaud Dunand's MICHELIN-starred Alpine French restaurant inside a chalet-style townhouse in Sathorn.
summaryLong:
Arnaud Dunand spent years running a two-MICHELIN-star kitchen at the Mandarin Oriental Bangkok before opening his own restaurant in 2022. Maison Dunand is unmistakably his project: the dining room is shaped like a Savoyard chalet, complete with timber and stone, and the menu is rooted in his native French Alps.

Dishes show off the Dunand technique — long-cooked sauces, classical garnishes, immaculate fish cookery — with a few Thai-pantry detours when the local market makes it irresistible. The wine list is a deep cut of Burgundy and the Rhône, with a discreet selection of Alpine whites that pair beautifully with the menu's lake-fish moments.

One MICHELIN star, awarded in 2023 and retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: Bangkok's most personal Alpine-French dining room — a former Mandarin two-star executive chef now cooking in his own house.
tags: michelin, 1-star, french, alpine, arnaud dunand, sathorn, chef-owned

title: Saneh Jaan
summaryShort: A one-MICHELIN-star contemporary Thai fine-dining restaurant inspired by the Thai-colonial style of King Rama V's era, set in the Sindhorn Tower.
summaryLong:
Saneh Jaan ('charming heart') is an attempt to bring full classical Thai dining back into the modern fine-dining conversation. The room references King Rama V the Great's era — dark woods, Thai architectural prints, low lighting — and the menu is built almost entirely from royal- and central-region recipes, executed without dilution.

The restaurant is firm about its identity: severe allergies are explicitly hard to accommodate because the kitchen leans on shrimp paste, fish sauce and coconut milk as foundational elements, and they are not going to change them. That confidence is part of why Saneh Jaan keeps its star year after year.

One MICHELIN star, retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: An uncompromised classical-Thai fine-dining room that refuses to soften its flavours for international palates.
priceNote: Set menus and à la carte; expect ~THB 2,500-4,500++ per person
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, classical thai, royal, sindhorn, fine dining

title: Samrub Samrub Thai
summaryShort: Prin Polsuk and Mint Jarukittikun's intimate one-MICHELIN-star kitchen in a 4-storey Si Lom townhouse — Thai cooking served as a single curated 'samrub' tray.
summaryLong:
Chef Prin Polsuk spent years as Thompson's senior lieutenant at Nahm before opening Samrub Samrub Thai with his wife Thanyaporn 'Mint' Jarukittikun. The name refers to the traditional Thai 'samrub' — a coordinated set of dishes meant to be eaten together over rice.

The restaurant occupies a small, white, four-storey renovated house just off Soi Yommarat. Each evening's menu is a single samrub: a curry, a stir-fry, a salad, a soup, a relish, all balanced against each other and against the rice. The format is austere by design, and Prin pushes regional dishes that wouldn't normally make it onto a fine-dining menu.

Bookings are run almost entirely through Line and Instagram DMs in addition to TableCheck. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: A chef who trained at Nahm running his own room with no compromise — among Thai food obsessives, the most credible one-star in Bangkok.
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, samrub, prin polsuk, si lom, fine dining

title: Signature Bangkok
summaryShort: Former two-MICHELIN-star French chef Thierry Drapeau's intimate Bangkok restaurant — a one-star floral-cuisine tasting menu on the 11th floor of VIE Hotel.
summaryLong:
Thierry Drapeau ran a two-MICHELIN-star restaurant in his native Vendée before relocating his project to Bangkok at VIE Hotel, an MGallery property in Ratchathewi. The cuisine is unapologetically French and built on classical jus-based sauce work, but Drapeau's signature is his use of edible flowers and delicate herbs — many sourced from boutique Thai growers — to add precise aromatic dimensions to each plate.

The Flower Bouquet set menus rotate with the seasons and have become the marquee offering. Service is limited to roughly twenty seats per evening; advance reservations are required and the kitchen plans the menu against confirmed bookings.

One MICHELIN star, retained for a fourth consecutive year in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: A former two-star French chef running a 20-seat dining room with a uniquely floral, herb-forward cuisine.
tags: michelin, 1-star, french, floral cuisine, thierry drapeau, vie hotel

title: etcha
summaryShort: Chef Giacomo Primante's debut one-MICHELIN-star tasting-menu room at Chatrium Grand Bangkok — European technique, Thai pantry, eleven courses.
summaryLong:
etcha is one of the standout new entries in the 2026 MICHELIN Guide Thailand. Italian chef Giacomo Primante curates a 'borderless dining' experience that combines European technique with Thai ingredients, served in a calm taupe-toned room with handmade pottery and golden cutlery.

The headline 11-course '360°' tasting and shorter eight-course '180°' menu both lean heavily on seasonal local produce: Andaman seafood, Northern Thai herbs, Issan-region beef. Plating is precise and quietly photographic; the kitchen is open to the room so you can watch each pass build.

The restaurant is on the 7th floor of Chatrium Grand Bangkok in Ratchathewi. Bookings require a full deposit at confirmation. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: One of the freshest one-star kitchens in the 2026 guide, with a chef-driven format that doesn't fit neatly into French, Italian or Thai categories.
tags: michelin, 1-star, borderless, european-thai, chatrium, new

title: Chim by Siam Wisdom
summaryShort: Chef Thanintorn 'Noom' Chantharawan's one-MICHELIN-star Thai tasting menu in a 100-year-old colonial-style house with a secluded back garden in Dusit.
summaryLong:
Chim by Siam Wisdom is the senior project of Chef Noom (Thanintorn Chantharawan), who has spent years researching dishes from the Rattanakosin period — the era of early 19th-century Bangkok. The setting matches the project: a 100-year-old house off Soi Ongkharak 13, with a secluded garden you walk through on the way in.

Menus are presented as themed sets that reference particular reigns or palace cooking traditions; the current 'Bicentennial Celebration Menu' running through early 2026 marks the 200-year anniversary of Bangkok with dishes pulled from period sources. Plating is composed and theatrical without falling into kitsch.

One MICHELIN star, retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: A Thai history lesson disguised as a tasting menu, in one of the most atmospheric private-villa dining rooms in the city.
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, rattanakosin, heritage, dusit, fine dining

title: Blue by Alain Ducasse
summaryShort: Alain Ducasse's only Bangkok restaurant, on the riverside terrace of IconSiam — modern French fine dining with one MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Blue is Alain Ducasse's solo Bangkok project, opened on the river-facing first floor of the ICONLUXE wing of IconSiam. Executive chef Evens López runs the kitchen, with the Ducasse atelier system supplying technique, recipe research and sourcing standards from Paris.

The cuisine is recognisably Ducasse: precise, polished, modern French with a strong Mediterranean undercurrent. Tasting menus are anchored by classics from the wider Ducasse repertoire (the bouillabaisse-inspired course, a duck-press dish, the chocolate-and-coffee finishers) re-engineered around Andaman seafood and Thai vegetables where it makes sense.

The room itself, by Jouin Manku, is one of the most striking French dining rooms in the region: cobalt-blue lacquer, arched ceilings, full-height river windows. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The only Ducasse restaurant in Thailand, in a Jouin Manku room with full Chao Phraya views — the platonic ideal of a riverside French fine-dining night.
priceNote: Set menus ~THB 4,500++ at lunch, ~THB 7,500++ at dinner
tags: michelin, 1-star, french, alain ducasse, iconsiam, riverside

title: Sushi Saito Bangkok
neighborhood: Charoenkrung (Four Seasons Hotel Bangkok at Chao Phraya)
summaryShort: The Bangkok branch of Tokyo legend Takashi Saito's sushi atelier, set inside Four Seasons Bangkok on the river. One MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Sushi Saito's flagship in Tokyo is one of the most sought-after omakase counters in the world. The Bangkok outpost, on the riverside campus of Four Seasons Hotel Bangkok at Chao Phraya, is the only Sushi Saito outside Japan and Hong Kong.

The counter team is trained directly in Saito's house style: rice tempered to body temperature, sharper-than-typical akazu vinegar seasoning, and otsumami starters that change with what flies in from Toyosu several times a week. Lunch tends to focus on the nigiri sequence, while dinner adds longer otsumami progressions.

The room is small (counter-only, around twelve seats per service) and the lunch and dinner sittings are tightly time-blocked. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: The only Sushi Saito-trained counter in Thailand, with fish flown from Toyosu several times a week — the highest-clearance edomae omakase in Bangkok.
tags: michelin, 1-star, japanese, sushi, omakase, edomae, four seasons, riverside

title: GOAT BKK
summaryShort: Chef Tan-Parkorn Kosiyapong's tasting-menu kitchen in a Sino-Portuguese house in Ekkamai, awarded its first MICHELIN star in 2025.
summaryLong:
GOAT opened in late 2020 and earned its first MICHELIN star in the 2025 guide. The restaurant is set in a Sino-Portuguese-styled shophouse in Ekkamai — a nod to Chef Tan-Parkorn Kosiyapong's own Thai-Chinese heritage and to Phuket's Hokkien diaspora.

The headline menu, 'From Land, Sea & Soil,' is a 12-course tasting that uses 100% Thai ingredients across a seasonal arc, with a quiet network of small farmers and fishers behind every course. Cooking technique merges Chinese banquet-kitchen moves (wok, smoking, steaming) with European stocks and Thai-herb finishing.

One MICHELIN star, retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: A 100%-Thai-ingredient sourcing standard plus a Thai-Chinese-Phuket DNA you don't see anywhere else in fine dining in the city.
tags: michelin, 1-star, modern thai, thai-chinese, ekkamai, sustainability

title: HAOMA
summaryShort: Chef Deepanker 'DK' Khosla's one-MICHELIN-star Neo-Indian restaurant — Thailand's first urban-farm and zero-waste fine-dining kitchen.
summaryLong:
HAOMA is built around DK Khosla's commitment to closed-loop dining. The site at Sukhumvit 31 includes a working aquaponic farm that grows herbs, leaves and a portion of the vegetables that hit the menu, and a strict zero-waste protocol governs the rest of the supply chain.

The cuisine is Neo-Indian: a tasting menu that travels regions across the subcontinent but expressed through technique-forward modern plating, with consistently strong vegetarian options (including a full vegetarian tasting). DK was named a Worlds 50 Best Champions of Change in 2021 for the kitchen's pandemic-era community work.

One MICHELIN star, retained in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: Thailand's first true farm-to-table fine-dining kitchen, run by one of the most credibly sustainability-driven chefs working in Asia.
priceNote: Tasting menus from ~THB 3,500++; vegetarian tasting available
tags: michelin, 1-star, indian, neo-indian, sustainability, urban farm, vegetarian-friendly

title: Juksunchae
summaryShort: An 11-course modern Korean tasting menu in a townhouse on Sukhumvit 49 — among the first Korean restaurants in Bangkok to hold a MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Juksunchae is the rare Korean fine-dining room in Bangkok that takes the full tasting-menu approach seriously. Its 11-course menu reads like a quiet master-class in Korean technique: deeply made stocks, long-fermented banchan, charcoal-grilled mains, and a closing rice course that recasts familiar comfort food as a fine-dining moment.

The dining space is calm and modern — pale wood, ceramic ware sourced from Korean potters, a small open kitchen at the rear. The wine and soju lists are unusually thoughtful, with a focus on natural Korean producers that you would have to fly to Seoul to find.

One MICHELIN star, awarded in the 2026 guide.
whySpecial: The most ambitious Korean tasting-menu room in Bangkok and one of very few one-star Korean restaurants in South-East Asia.
tags: michelin, 1-star, korean, modern korean, sukhumvit, fine dining

title: AKKEE Thai Delicacies & Tasting Counter
cuisine: Heritage Thai (no electricity / no modern equipment)
summaryShort: Sittikorn Chantop's one-MICHELIN-star Thai counter in Nonthaburi — recipes drawn from antique cookbooks and cooked without modern kitchen equipment.
summaryLong:
AKKEE may be the most unusual one-star kitchen in the wider Bangkok region. Chef Sittikorn Chantop and his sommelier brother Sivakorn ban modern electrical kitchen equipment from the cookline by design — every dish is cooked over fire and finished by hand, using recipes pulled from a personal collection of antique Thai cookery books.

The tasting counter focuses on Eastern-coast seafood (the menu is unsuitable for guests with seafood allergies or vegan/vegetarian needs) and is paired with a deep low-intervention, biodynamic and organic wine list, plus the famous AKKEE draught-beer flights.

The room is in Nonthaburi, just north of central Bangkok. One MICHELIN star.
whySpecial: An anti-modernist Thai kitchen — no induction, no immersion circulators, no shortcuts — running an antique-cookbook menu nobody else is doing.
tags: michelin, 1-star, thai, heritage, no-electricity, nonthaburi, seafood

title: Cannubi by Umberto Bombana
summaryShort: Three-MICHELIN-star Italian chef Umberto Bombana's Bangkok project at Dusit Thani, the first Italian restaurant in Thailand to hold a MICHELIN star.
summaryLong:
Cannubi opened with the relaunched Dusit Thani Bangkok and immediately became the first Italian restaurant in Thailand to hold a MICHELIN star (one star in the 2026 guide). The kitchen is led by chef Andrea Susto, with Hong-Kong-based three-star chef Umberto Bombana — of 8 ½ Otto e Mezzo Bombana — overseeing the menu direction.

The menu is unmistakably Bombana: Piedmontese roots, generous use of Italian seasonal produce (white truffle in season, Sicilian seafood, Lombard cheeses), and a deep, unfussy classical technique. The dining room sits on the lower floors of the new Dusit Thani tower, with a wine bar attached for aperitivo and grappa service.

One MICHELIN star, awarded in 2026.
whySpecial: The first Italian restaurant in Thailand to hold a MICHELIN star, with menu direction from one of the world's most decorated Italian chefs.
tags: michelin, 1-star, italian, piedmont, umberto bombana, dusit thani, new
